overview.body: This is a MASTER week! You'll copy your Lab 1.2 code and add nested decisions. When a player reaches Houston, they get a second choice: continue to Galveston for the beach, or stay put. This is an if statement inside another if statement — decisions within decisions!

mentorQuote.quote: This is where it gets interesting! Nested if statements let you build decision trees — like a 'choose your own adventure' book. If you reach Houston AND you have miles left AND you want to continue... then you can try for Galveston. That's three levels of logic working together!

objectives: Write a nested if inside the Houston branch to ask about Galveston
Use the or operator to accept both 'y' and 'Y'
Set a status variable (WINNER, SHORT, DETOUR, STOPPED, ERROR) in every branch
Display a final message based on the status variable
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 3: Decision Structures and Boolean Logic

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_CORPUS
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Corpus Christi (150 miles)

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_HOUSTON
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Houston (200 miles)

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_AUSTIN
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Austin (80 miles)

namedConstants.name: HOU_TO_GALVESTON
namedConstants.description: Distance from Houston to Galveston (50 miles) — back from Lab 1.1!

programRequirements: Your program MUST start from your Lab 1.2 code
Your program MUST have a nested if inside the Houston branch
Your program MUST use the or operator to accept 'y' or 'Y'
Your program MUST set total_distance and status in every branch
Your program MUST display a final message based on status
Your program MUST include your docstring with name, abc123, section, and description

rows.task: Copy 1.2, add constant, add tracking vars
The big one: nested if with Galveston
Corpus, Austin, Invalid with new vars

timeGuide.strategy: Draw it out! Before coding, sketch the decision tree on paper. Houston alone has 4 possible outcomes: didn't make it, made it but stayed, made it and reached Galveston, or fell short of Galveston. Visualizing helps you track all the branches.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. You're starting from Lab 1.2 — the [NEW] and [REPLACED] tags show what changes this week.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150
SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200
SAT_TO_AUSTIN = 80
HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50    [NEW-CP1]

# ---- MAIN PROGRAM ----
def main():
│
│── Welcome banner, player name, roll
│── miles_remaining = miles_rolled      [NEW-CP1]
│── total_distance = 0                  [NEW-CP1]
│
│── Display menu, get choice
│
│── if choice == 1 (Corpus):            [REPLACED-CP3]
│       check miles_remaining >= 0
│       set status WINNER or SHORT
│       set total_distance
│
│── elif choice == 2 (Houston):         [REPLACED-CP2]
│       check miles_remaining >= 0
│       if made it:
│       │   ask 'Continue to Galveston? (y/n)'
│       │   if answer == 'y' or answer == 'Y':
│       │       check remaining vs HOU_TO_GALVESTON
│       │       status = WINNER or SHORT
│       │   else:
│       │       status = STOPPED
│       else:
│           status = SHORT
│       set total_distance
│
│── elif choice == 3 (Austin):          [REPLACED-CP3]
│       check miles_remaining >= 0
│       status = DETOUR or SHORT
│       set total_distance
│
│── else (invalid):                     [REPLACED-CP3]
│       status = ERROR
│
│── Display TRIP SUMMARY
│── if status == 'WINNER': ...          [NEW-CP4]
│── elif status == 'SHORT': ...
│── elif status == 'DETOUR': ...
│── else: ...

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

content.body: A foundation with tracking variables that will accumulate totals as the player travels through multiple cities.
Tracking variables that accumulate values across a program are everywhere in real software: shopping carts, game scores, step counters. Learning to initialize and update them is fundamental.

items: Copy your Lab 1.2 code and save as lab-1-3.py
Update your docstring with the new lab number and description
Add the Galveston constant with your other constants: HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50

references.description: Shows a nested if — an if inside another if. Study the indentation carefully. You're building on Lab 1.2 patterns while preparing for the nested structure in Checkpoint 2.

rows: Wrong initial value for tracking vars
total_distance starts at 0; miles_remaining starts at miles_rolled
NameError when you try to use it in Checkpoint 2
Placement matters: tracking vars must be initialized before the if-elif-else chain

content.body: Step 1: Add debug lines after your new variables:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE AFTER TESTING
    print(f'DEBUG: miles_remaining = {miles_remaining}')
    print(f'DEBUG: total_distance = {total_distance}')

content.title: Delete debug lines
content.body: Delete debug lines before moving to Checkpoint 2.
The heart of this assignment: if the player reaches Houston, they can choose to continue to Galveston. This is an if inside an if — a nested if.

references.description: Shows nested if — the inner if only runs when the outer if is true. Study how the indentation levels work. That's exactly what you need here.
Shows the or operator in conditions. The pattern you need: choice == 'y' or choice == 'Y' — you must repeat the full comparison on both sides.

content.quote: When I first learned the or operator, I kept writing 'if choice == "y" or "Y":' and wondering why it always ran. You need the full comparison on both sides: 'choice == "y" or choice == "Y"'. Trust me, this trips everyone up at first!

content.body: Replace your Houston branch (elif choice == 2) with nested logic. The structure is:

items: Check if made it to Houston (miles_remaining >= SAT_TO_HOUSTON)
If yes: subtract SAT_TO_HOUSTON from miles_remaining, set total_distance, then ask 'Continue to Galveston? (y/n): '
If they say y/Y: check if miles_remaining >= HOU_TO_GALVESTON — set status WINNER or SHORT accordingly
If they say anything else: set status to 'STOPPED'
If didn't make Houston: set status to 'SHORT', set total_distance to SAT_TO_HOUSTON

rows: Always True! Need: choice == 'y' or choice == 'Y'
Nested code must be indented further than the outer if
Must subtract SAT_TO_HOUSTON before checking if you can reach Galveston

items: Roll 20, choice 2, 'y': Made Houston, reached Galveston → WINNER
Roll 15, choice 2, 'y': Made Houston, short of Galveston → SHORT
Nested if inside the Houston branch
Logic handling y/Y for continue, anything else for stop

content.body: Updated Corpus, Austin, and Invalid branches that set total_distance and status so the final summary has accurate data.
For each remaining branch, ensure you set both tracking variables:

content.title: Status Values
items: SHORT — Ran out of miles before the destination
DETOUR — Made it to Austin (no beach there!)

references.description: Shows setting different variables in each branch of an if-elif-else. Same pattern here — each destination sets its own status and total_distance.

rows: 'Winner' vs 'WINNER' — be consistent, Python is case-sensitive
status stays undefined, causing a NameError in the summary

content.body: Step 1: Add a debug line at the END of the full if-elif-else block (right before the summary):

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE AFTER TESTING
    print(f'DEBUG: total_distance = {total_distance}, status = {status}')

Nested if inside the Houston branch
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 2 and 3: all Chapter 2 and 3 techniques including nested if statements, logical operators (or, and, not), abs(), and string comparisons. NOT permitted: loops, lists, functions you define, or any Chapter 4+ techniques. Note: The main() structure is a required template you copy exactly — you'll learn why in Chapter 5.

permittedTechniques.notPermitted: loops, lists, functions you define, or any Chapter 4+ techniques. Note: The main() structure is a required template — you'll learn why in Chapter 5.

ifYouGetStuck: Draw the decision tree on paper first — Houston has 4 paths, sketch them all before coding
Check your indentation carefully — nested code must be indented further than the outer if
Test one branch at a time using the debug technique from earlier checkpoints
Check Program 3-5 (loan_qualifier.py) for the nested if pattern
Submit to CodeGrade early and often to see which tests pass
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapters 2–3 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example, your primary reference
